rulings.Rulings: The Court grants Respondents' Request for Judicial Notice. Notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in pleadings or transcripts. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) Respondent and Trustee Jeanne Kwong's Motion for Summary Adjudication of the Second Amended Petition is denied. Under CCP section 437c(f)(1), "A motion for summary adjudication shall be granted only if it completely disposes of a cause of action, an affirmative defense, a claim for damages, or an issue of duty." Even assuming, arguendo, that there is a valid statute of limitations defense under Probate Code section 16460 to the breach of duty and constructive fraud claims arising from the acts disclosed in the Seventh Accounting, summary adjudication of these claims would not dispose of an entire "cause of action, an affirmative defense, a claim for damages, or an issue of duty" pleaded in the Second Amended Petition. Were the Court to grant this motion, the pleaded causes of action and damage claims for breach of duty and constructive fraud would remain, based on the other allegations and the actions described in the Eighth and Ninth Accountings - which the motion does not challenge. At most, granting the requested summary adjudication would merely exclude a category of evidence rather than a cause of action. (See Hindin v. Rust (2004) 118 Cal.App.4th 1247, 1255 [in malicious prosecution case, trial court erred in granting defendant summary adjudication on ground that defendant had probable cause to bring one of its two claims in underlying case because that determination did not dispose of plaintiff's entire cause of action].) Unlike in Lilienthal & Fowler v. Superior Court (1993) 12 Cal. App. 4th 1848, 1854, cited in the Reply, here damages are not sought based on "matters [that] have no relation to each other" and which are "distinct and separate." The Second Amended Petition alleges breach of the duty of loyalty, breach of the duty of care, and constructive fraud based on allegations of years of mismanagement and intentional malfeasance committed by Trustee to the detriment of the beneficiary, up to and including the time periods covered by the Seventh, Eighth, and Ninth accountings, and as allegedly evidenced by the accountings. In light of the rationale for this ruling, it is not necessary to reach Petitioner's relation-back, tolling, and equitable estoppel arguments. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on October 14, 2020. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate.
